questions.question: Explain the difference between shallow copy and deep copy with respect to copy constructors in C++. When is a deep copy necessary?
keyPoints: Shallow copy copies member values directly, potentially creating dangling pointers
Deep copy allocates new memory and copies pointed-to content
Deep copy required when class contains pointers to dynamically allocated memory
Default copy constructor performs shallow copy

questions.question: What is the purpose of static data members in C++ classes? Explain with an example how static members differ from instance members.
keyPoints: Static members belong to class, not individual objects
Shared across all objects of the class
Must be initialized outside class definition
Accessed using class name or object with :: operator

questions.question: (a) Explain the concept of const member functions and const objects in C++. How does mutable keyword help in modifying const objects? (b) Discuss the differences between public, private, and protected access specifiers in the context of inheritance, illustrating with examples of each inheritance type.
keyPoints: Const member functions cannot modify object state (except mutable members)
Const objects can only call const member functions
Private: inaccessible to derived classes and outside code; Protected: accessible to derived classes; Public: fully accessible
Inheritance types determine base class member visibility in derived class

questions.question: (a) Explain the need for initializer lists in C++ constructors. When are they mandatory versus recommended? (b) Discuss operator overloading in C++. How does it differ from function overloading? Explain with an example of overloading the insertion (<<) and addition (+) operators for a user-defined class.
keyPoints: Initializer lists required for const members, references, and base class constructors
More efficient than assignment in constructor body (avoids extra default construction)
Operator overloading extends meaning of operators for user-defined types
Operators must be non-static member functions or friend functions; cannot change precedence/arity
